instructions: Speak like a friendly, confident cybersecurity expert explaining to a colleague. Clear and measured pace, natural emphasis on key terms, calm and authoritative — not rushed, not robotic.
intro: Let's secure a real virtual machine — and not a tidy one. This is juice-shop, a deliberately vulnerable demo app, and it's exposed in ways that should make you wince. We'll walk its networking, its disk encryption, its threat protection, and its patching, and turn it from a target into something defensible.

bullets: The overview shows the VM's public IP, OS, size, and resource group — the first things an attacker also sees.
A public IP plus open management ports equals an internet-reachable target. juice-shop has both.
Treat every VM as breach-able: minimize exposure, encrypt at rest, detect threats, and patch fast.
sections.narration: This is the overview for juice-shop, a deliberately vulnerable demo VM. Right here you can read its public IP address, operating system, size, and resource group — and that's exactly the information an attacker enumerates first. The moment a VM has a public IP and open management ports, it becomes an internet-reachable target. So our job across the next few blades is simple: shrink the attack surface, encrypt the disks, turn on threat detection, and keep it patched. Let's start where the danger usually lives — the network.

sections.cardTitle: NSG: SSH open to the whole internet
bullets: The network security group juice-shop-nsg controls inbound traffic to the VM's NIC and subnet.
Look at the inbound rules: port 22 (SSH) is allowed from Source '*' — that's 0.0.0.0/0, the entire internet. Ports 443, 8080 and 6001 are wide open too.
NEVER expose SSH or RDP to '*'. Scope management ports to your IP or a bastion, and let an explicit allow-then-deny rule shut the rest.
sections.narration: This is the networking blade, and it shows the inbound rules from the network security group, the N.S.G., named juice-shop-nsg. Here's the classic mistake, highlighted for you: port twenty-two, S.S.H., is allowed from source star — and star means zero-dot-zero-dot-zero-dot-zero slash zero, the entire internet. Ports four-forty-three, eighty-eighty, and six-thousand-one are wide open too. This is how VMs get brute-forced and owned. The rule is absolute: never expose S.S.H. or R.D.P. to star. Scope your management ports to a single trusted I.P. address or, better, put them behind Azure Bastion so the VM needs no public management port at all. Remember N.S.G. rules are evaluated by priority, lowest number first, so a tight allow rule followed by a deny-all is how you lock this down.

sections.cardTitle: Disks: SSE, CMK & encryption at host
bullets: Server-side encryption (SSE) is always on — every managed disk is AES-256 encrypted at rest with platform-managed keys, for free.
For key control, use a customer-managed key (CMK) from Key Vault via a disk encryption set — you rotate it, you can revoke it.
Encryption at host extends SSE to the temp disk and disk caches end-to-end; it's the modern choice over the retiring Azure Disk Encryption (ADE).
sections.narration: On the disks blade you control encryption at rest. Start with the good news: server-side encryption is always on, so every managed disk is already encrypted with two-fifty-six-bit A.E.S. using platform-managed keys, at no cost. But platform-managed keys are controlled by Microsoft. For real key control, switch to a customer-managed key — a C.M.K. — stored in Key Vault and wired in through a disk encryption set; now you own rotation and you can revoke access. To go further, enable encryption at host: that extends encryption to the temporary disk and the disk caches, giving you end-to-end coverage without touching the V.M.'s C.P.U. Encryption at host is the modern choice — Azure Disk Encryption, the older in-guest approach, is heading for retirement.

focus.text: Defender for Servers is OFF — JIT is the upgrade to enable

bullets: Microsoft Defender for Servers is currently OFF on this subscription — so there's no threat detection or hardening on juice-shop yet. This is the upgrade to turn on.
Plan 2 unlocks Just-in-Time (JIT) VM access: management ports stay closed and open only on request, from your IP, for a short, audited window.
JIT directly fixes the port-22-from-'*' problem — instead of a standing open door, access is granted on demand from the Connect blade.
sections.narration: Now the upgrade that ties the network story together. Microsoft Defender for Servers is currently turned off on this subscription, which means juice-shop has no real-time threat detection and no hardening recommendations yet. If we enable Defender for Servers Plan two, we unlock Just-in-Time V.M. access — J.I.T. With J.I.T., your management ports stay closed by default and open only when you request them, only from your I.P. address, only for a short, fully audited window. Think about what that does to the problem we saw earlier: instead of port twenty-two standing open to the whole internet, S.S.H. is a door that's locked until you ask, then automatically re-locked. You'd request that access right here from the Connect experience. Defender for Servers also adds vulnerability scanning and alerting on top — exactly what a vulnerable box like this needs.

bullets: Azure Update Manager assesses and patches Windows and Linux VMs from one place — no agent juggling, with periodic 24-hour assessment.
Set a patch orchestration mode (e.g. customer-managed schedules or Azure-managed safe deployment) and a maintenance window so updates land predictably.
Pair patching with a vulnerability assessment solution; prioritize internet-facing VMs like this one first — exposure plus unpatched equals incident.
sections.narration: Finally, patching — because an exposed, unpatched VM is just a countdown. On the updates blade you reach Azure Update Manager, which assesses and installs updates for both Windows and Linux machines from a single place, and you can turn on periodic assessment so it re-checks every twenty-four hours. Choose a patch orchestration mode — customer-managed schedules, or Azure-managed safe deployment — and define a maintenance window so updates land when you expect them, not in the middle of a workload. Then pair this with a vulnerability assessment solution, and always prioritize your internet-facing machines first. For a box like juice-shop, that's everything: it's exposed, so the window between a patch being released and you applying it is exactly the window an attacker uses.

card.title: VM security in five rules
lines: 1 · Never expose SSH/RDP to '*' — scope NSGs or use Bastion.
2 · Encryption at rest is on; add CMK + encryption at host.
3 · Turn on Defender for Servers for detection & hardening.
4 · Use Just-in-Time access — ports open on demand only.
5 · Patch fast with Update Manager; internet-facing VMs first.

recap.narration: In short: never leave S.S.H. or R.D.P. open to star — scope your N.S.G. rules tightly or front them with Bastion; remember encryption at rest is always on, then add a customer-managed key and encryption at host for full control; turn on Defender for Servers so you actually get threat detection and hardening; use Just-in-Time access so management ports open only on demand; and patch quickly with Update Manager, prioritizing the internet-facing machines first. Do that, and juice-shop stops being an easy target.
recap.examTip: Exam tip: know NSG rule evaluation by priority and that Source '*' means the whole internet; SSE versus encryption at host versus the retiring Azure Disk Encryption; and that Just-in-Time VM access requires Microsoft Defender for Servers Plan 2.

exercises.title: Exercise 1 — Close the open management port
lines: GOAL · Stop SSH being open to the internet
1 · VM → Networking → open the NSG; find the inbound rule for port 22 from Source '*'
2 · Edit it: change Source to your own IP (or delete it and add a Bastion-only rule)
3 · Add a low-priority Deny-all inbound rule beneath your explicit allows
✓ DONE WHEN · IP flow verify shows port 22 denied from any IP except yours
exercises.narration: In your own lab, take a VM with a public IP and open the networking blade. Find the inbound N.S.G. rule that allows port twenty-two from source star, and fix it: change the source to your own I.P. address, or delete it entirely and route S.S.H. through Azure Bastion instead. Then add a low-priority deny-all inbound rule underneath your explicit allow rules, so anything you didn't expressly permit is blocked. You're done when you run I.P. flow verify in Network Watcher and it shows port twenty-two denied from every address except yours.

exercises.title: Exercise 2 — Defender, JIT & encryption
lines: GOAL · Turn on-demand access and full disk encryption on
1 · Enable Microsoft Defender for Servers Plan 2 on the subscription
2 · Enable Just-in-Time VM access; request SSH for a 1-hour window from your IP
3 · On Disks, deallocate the VM and enable encryption at host (CMK optional)
✓ DONE WHEN · Port 22 is closed until a JIT request opens it, and encryption at host is On
exercises.narration: Second exercise: layer on detection and encryption. Enable Microsoft Defender for Servers Plan two on your subscription, then turn on Just-in-Time V.M. access for the machine and request S.S.H. for a one-hour window from your own I.P. — watch the temporary N.S.G. rule appear and then expire. Next, on the disks blade, deallocate the V.M. and enable encryption at host; optionally point it at a customer-managed key from Key Vault. You're done when port twenty-two stays closed until a J.I.T. request opens it, and encryption at host shows as On.
